title: Want More Babies? We Need More Friends | Institute for Family Studies
gist: A City Journal excerpt uses a 1775 Harvard forecast of 1.28 billion Americans by 2000 — which reality undershot by about a billion — to frame declining fertility, arguing rebuilding friendship is key to raising birth rates.

insights.claim: In 1775 Harvard professor Edward Wigglesworth projected that the American colonies, then doubling every 20 years on a high birth rate, would reach 1.28 billion people by 2000.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Anchors how far actual US population fell short of fertility-driven expectations, dramatizing the long-run demographic shortfall.

insights.claim: By the year 2000 the US population came in roughly a billion short of Wigglesworth's 1.28 billion projection.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Quantifies the cumulative demographic gap that fertility decline has produced over 225 years.
insights.quote: When the year 2000 arrived, we came in about a billion short.

insights.claim: The piece argues, per its title, that rebuilding friendship and social ties is central to raising American birth rates.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Reinforces the IFS thesis that social-network revival, not just cash, is a lever on fertility.
insights.locator: Want More Babies? We Need More Friends
